elements.sent-2411-001: The main assumptions of this technique are that the gas is in a low-excitation state ($x_\text{e < 0.3$) and the equilibrium of the underlying ionisation network is reached.}
elements.sent-2411-002: In addition, we investigate how a non-equilibrium state of the gas and the presence of extinction influence the BE99 technique.
elements.sent-2411-003: We apply the BE99 method on synthetic spectra also in the non-equilibrium state.
elements.sent-2411-004: We extent the BE99 technique to higher ionisation fractions by considering additional reactions, which involve higher ionisation states of oxygen, nitrogen, and sulphur.
elements.sent-2411-005: We test our concepts on the low excitation outflow of Par Lup 3-4 and the high excitation 244-440 Proplyd in Orion.

elements.sent-2411-007: We find that the BE99 equilibrium is reached faster than the hydrogen recombination time.
elements.sent-2411-008: The application to the Par Lup 3-4 outflow shows that the classical BE99 lines together with the [N\,I]$\lambda\lambda$5198+5200 lines do not meet in one location in the ($x_\text{e , T_\text{e}$)-diagram.}
elements.sent-2411-009: This indicates that the gas parameters derived from the classical BE99 method are not fully consistent with other observed line ratios.
elements.sent-2411-010: A multi-line approach is necessary to determine the gas parameters.
elements.sent-2411-011: From our analysis we derive $n_e \sim 45\,000\,\text{cm ^{-3 -53\,000\,\text{cm ^{-3}$, $T_e = 7\,600\,\text{K -8\,000\,\text{K}$, and $x_e \sim 0.027-0.036$ for the Par Lup 3-4 outflow.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: For the 244-440 Proplyd we were able to use the line ratios of [S\,II]$\lambda\lambda$6716+6731, [O\,I]$\lambda\lambda$6300+6363, and [OII]$\lambda\lambda$7320, 7330 in the BE99 diagram to estimate the ionisation fraction at knot E3 ($x_e = 0.58\pm 0.05$).
elements.sent-2411-013: Exploiting new line ratios reveals more insights on the state of the gas.
elements.sent-2411-014: Our analysis indicates, however, that a multi-line approach is more robust in deriving gas parameters, especially for high density gas.
elements.sent-2411-015: Protostellar outflows are essential consequences when young stars are forming \citep[e.g.][]{frank_2014, bally_2016, pascucci_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: They transport away a substantial amount of excess angular momentum from the protostellar system and thus prevent the spinning up of the protostar.
elements.sent-2411-017: In order to fully understand the nature of their gas excitation and the launching mechanism it is important to accurately measure the physical conditions of these outflows, that is the electron density $n_e$, electron temperature $T_e$, and ionisation fraction $x_e$.
elements.sent-2411-018: These key parameters can be constrained observationally by analysing the emitted forbidden emission lines (FELs), which are attributed to the presence of shocks.
elements.sent-2411-019: Protostellar outflows are typically traced by FELs such as, e.g., [S\,II]$\lambda\lambda 4068, 4076$, [O\,I]$\lambda\lambda 6300, 6363$, [S\,II]$\lambda\lambda 6716, 6731$, and [N\,II]$\lambda\lambda 6548, 6583$ indicating low-excitation gas conditions ($x_e \lesssim 0.3$, $n_e \sim 10^2-10^6\,\text{cm ^{-3 , T_e \sim 5\,000-20\,000\,\text{K}$).}}
elements.sent-2411-020: In this context different methods are being used to derive the gas parameters: 1.
elements.sent-2411-021: Shock models can be used for comparing observed FELs with predictions \citep[e.g.][]{hollenbach_1989, hartigan_1994 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Although this approach is the natural choice as shocks cause the observed emission, this approach introduces a strong model-dependency and degeneracy.
elements.sent-2411-023: The geometry of the shock, the shock type (C-shock, J-shock, radiative shock) and other parameters (shock velocities, preshock densities, magnetic fields) substantially influence the resulting FEL spectra; 2.
elements.sent-2411-024: Individual line ratios that are sensitive to one or more gas parameters can be used as diagnostic tools \citep[e.g.][]{osterbrock_book, natta_2014, fang_2018, nisini_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: In this framework the extinction and the gas parameters are typically constrained separately and/or consecutively -- depending on the available line ratios.
elements.sent-2411-026: For example, electron densities and temperatures are typically inferred from the [S\,II]$\lambda$6716/[S\,II]$\lambda$6731 or [O\,I]$\lambda\lambda$ (6300+6363)/[O\,I]$\lambda$5577 line ratios, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-027: The ionisation fraction is then derived from further line ratios (e.g. [N\,II]$\lambda$6583/[O\,I]$\lambda$6300) under the assumption of a certain ionisation model with $n_e$ and $T_e$ as previously determined parameters.
elements.sent-2411-028: This method is in essence a multi-step approach.
elements.sent-2411-029: Often only a range for $n_e$, $T_e$, and $x_e$ can be constrained and some available lines remain unused.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-030: Another approach is to find the best excitation model, whereby again no detailed shock physics is involved -- that describes simultaneously all observed line ratios that are attributed to the same emitting gas \citep[e.g.][]{hartigan_2007, giannini_2015, giannini_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Mathematically, this represents a nonlinear minimisation problem.
elements.sent-2411-032: Model grid calculations are usually preferred over model fitting, which in turn can be computationally very extensive already for a handful of parameters and a grid with moderate resolution.
elements.sent-2411-033: As a caveat, not all appearing lines can be blindly combined, as they can come from different gas phases.. 4.
elements.sent-2411-034: A compromise between computationally expensive grid calculations and an in-depth analysis of a few diagnostic diagrams is the BE99-method \citep{bacciotti_1999 , where only one diagnostic diagram is utilised to derive $n_e$, $T_e$, and $x_e$ \citep[e.g.][]{Nisini_2005, podio_2006, ray_2007, coffey_2008, podio_2009, mauri_2014 .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The BE99-method utilises six forbidden emission lines ([O\,I]$\lambda\lambda 6300, 6363$, [S\,II]$\lambda\lambda 6716, 6731$, and [N\,II]$\lambda\lambda 6548, 6583$) in the visual part of the electromagnetic spectrum and works in the framework of collisional ionisation equilibrium, where the atoms and ions are assumed to exist mostly in their ground state.
elements.sent-2411-036: In a nutshell, the BE99 diagnostic diagram is constructed as follows: From the observed six FELs three line ratios with their errors are calculated: O\,I/N\,II, O\,I/S\,II, and N\,II/S\,II (O\,I := [O\,I]$\lambda\lambda$6300+6363, N\,II := [N\,II]$\lambda\lambda$6548+6583, and S\,II := [S\,II]$\lambda\lambda$6716+6731).
elements.sent-2411-037: These three ratios are then compared with theoretical predictions in the $(x_e, T_e)$-parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-038: If the observed line ratio with error margins is consistent with a given set of values in the parameter space -- say $\{x_e^i, T_e^j\}$ -- this point is accepted as potential representation of the data.
elements.sent-2411-039: For all three line ratios these points are collected and effectively appear as three extended curves, that is stripes, in the $(x_e, T_e)$-parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-040: The location, where all stripes overlap, gives the determined gas parameters.
elements.sent-2411-041: All the above mentioned methods are based on time-independent models, that is they assume that the emitting gas is in equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-042: The hydrogen recombination time $\tau_\text{rec}$ or dynamical time scale $\tau_\text{dyn}$ are usually given as justification for their usage.
elements.sent-2411-043: Since protostellar outflows are not stationary -- as proper motions studies show -- these and potentially other time-scales play a crucial role.
elements.sent-2411-044: Furthermore, with new spectroscopic instruments such as XShooter, UVES, or MUSE not only the visual part of the spectrum can be studied but also other valuable emission lines that are connected to the BE99 lines in the blue or near-infrared are accessible.
elements.sent-2411-045: In turn, the BE99 method can be extended in a natural way, i.e. by including more emission line ratios as stripes in the ($x_\text{e , T_\text{e}$)-parameter space potentially providing more information about the present gas conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-046: In this study we explore the idea of extending the BE99 method by utilising additional line ratios in the spectral range of $3\,500-11\,000\,\AA$ and considering higher ionisation fractions ($x_\text{e > 0.3$).}
elements.sent-2411-047: Furthermore, we numerically solve a network of ionisation reactions, on which the BE99 ionisation balance is based on, in the time-domain to investigate how the non-equilibrium situation affects the BE99 method.
elements.sent-2411-048: We study the time scales of reaching the equilibrium to inspect how well the equilibrium assumption is justified when applied to protostellar outflows.
elements.sent-2411-049: We test our propositions on the low excitation outflow of Par Lup 3-4 and the high excitation 244-440 Proplyd in Orion.
elements.section-2411-002: The BE99 method in the time domain
elements.sent-2411-050: One of the main assumption of the BE99 method is that the reaction equilibrium in the involved atoms and ions is reached.
elements.sent-2411-051: In this Section we investigate the situation, when the BE99 ionisation balance is not yet reached.
elements.sent-2411-052: We use a simple network of ionisation reactions that lead to the equilibrium ionisation balance of the BE99 method.
elements.sent-2411-053: Four elements are involved in the BE99 technique.
elements.sent-2411-054: These are nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), sulphur (S), and hydrogen (H).
elements.sent-2411-055: Their relevant ionisation states are denoted as $Y^k \in \{ \text{N ^0, \text{N ^+, \text{O ^0, \text{O ^+, \text{S ^+, \text{H ^0, \text{H ^+ \}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Their number densities are denoted as $n(Y^k)$.
elements.sent-2411-058: The total number densities of an element $Y$, here introduced as $n_\text{tot (Y)$, are given by the sum of all relevant ionisation states.}
elements.sent-2411-059: In a low-excitation medium we expect sulphur to be mostly singly ionised.
elements.sent-2411-060: Nitrogen, oxygen, and hydrogen coexist in their neutral and singly ionised form simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-061: Thus, their total number densities and ionisation fractions are given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:sulfur} n_\text{tot}( Y) = n( Y^0) + n( Y^{+}),\end{equation} and \begin{equation}x_Y = n(Y^{+})/\left[ n(Y^0) + n(Y^{+})\right]. \label{eq:ionisation_fraction_N}\end{equation} Three atomic processes are considered in the BE99 technique: 1.

elements.sent-2411-063: Without this assumption a time dependent version of the BE99 method can be formulated from the underlying set of reactions, that is \begin{equation}\label{eq:reactions_A} \text{N}^0 +\text{H}^+ \underset{\delta_\text{N}'}{\stackrel{\delta_\text{N}}{\rightleftharpoons}} \text{N}^+ +\text{H}^0 (\text{charge exchange}) ,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\text{N}^0 +\text{e}^- \overset{C_\text{N}}{\longrightarrow} \text{N}^+ +2\text{e}^- (\text{coll. ionisation}),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\text{N}^+ +\text{e}^- \overset{\alpha_\text{N}}{\longrightarrow} \text{N}^0 (\text{recombination}),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\text{O}^0 +\text{H}^+ \underset{\delta_\text{O}'}{\stackrel{\delta_\text{O}}{\rightleftharpoons}} \text{O}^+ +\text{H}^0 (\text{charge exchange}) ,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\text{O}^0 +\text{e}^- \overset{C_\text{O}}{\longrightarrow} \text{O}^+ +2\text{e}^- (\text{coll. ionisation}),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\text{O}^+ +\text{e}^- \overset{\alpha_\text{O}}{\longrightarrow} \text{O}^0 (\text{recombination}),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\text{H}^0 +\text{e}^- \overset{C_\text{H}}{\longrightarrow} \text{H}^+ +2\text{e}^- (\text{coll. ionisation}),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:reactions_J} \text{H}^+ +\text{e}^- \overset{\alpha_\text{H}}{\longrightarrow} \text{H}^0 (\text{recombination}) .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-115: In the following, we therefore solve the ODEs numerically to study the how fast the equilibrium is reached.
elements.sent-2411-116: We define that the equilibrium is reached ($\tau_\text{eq}$), when the ionisation fractions of hydrogen, nitrogen, and oxygen stay constant with time and deviate only by 5\% from the ionisation equilibrium (Eqs.\,\ref{eq:BE99_EQUILIBRIUM_N and \ref{eq:BE99_EQUILIBRIUM_O ).}}
elements.sent-2411-117: With this definition we numerically found a crude estimate: \begin{equation}\label{eq:equilibrium_time_scale} \tau_\text{eq} \approx 10\,\text{yr} \,\frac{ x_\text{e}^{init}}{n_\text{e}^{init}\,[\text{cm}^{-3}]} \, \text{exp}\left(\frac{145528}{T_\text{e}\,[\text{K}]}\right).\end{equation}.

elements.subsection-2411-006: Resolving the BE99 method in the time domain
elements.sent-2411-128: The critical question for the applicability of the BE99 method may actually not be if the gas is in reaction equilibrium - this may never be the case in any outflow region - but instead how far off the gas is from the equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-129: In order to answer this question we follow the numerical solution of our model in the time domain.
elements.sent-2411-130: At each point in time we have full knowledge of the true gas conditions and can therefore calculate synthetic spectra in the non-equilibrium and equilibrium state.
elements.sent-2411-131: These (model-dependent) synthetic spectra can be used as input for the (model-independent) BE99 method.
elements.sent-2411-132: This situation corresponds to the observers perspective who does not know if the equilibrium is reached.
elements.sent-2411-133: Applying the BE99 technique out of equilibrium will give measurements of the electron density, temperature and ionisation fraction ($n_e^\text{BE99}$, $T_\text{e ^\text{BE99}$, $x_\text{e ^\text{BE99}$) that do not represent the true gas conditions ($n_\text{e ^\text{true}$, $T_\text{e ^\text{true}$, $x_\text{e ^\text{true}$).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: As the system evolves in time from a non-equilibrium to the equilibrium state the BE99 method will provide higher accuracy in these three gas parameters, i.e. it converges.
elements.sent-2411-135: In order to quantify this transition phase we introduce the time scale $\tau_\text{BE}$.
elements.sent-2411-136: We define $\tau_\text{BE}$ as the time at which the BE99 method provides a measurement of the ionisation fraction within 5\% of the true ionisation fraction, which is comparable to a typical measurement error of excellent observational data: \begin{equation}\label{condition_new_BE} \left\| 1 - x_\text{e}^\text{BE99}/x_\text{e}^\text{true} \right\| < 0.05 \quad \text{for} \quad t > \tau_\text{BE} .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-137: We visualise the convergence of the BE99 method in the time domain in Figure\,\ref{fig:BE_time_scale : already within $\tau_\text{BE \approx 10^{8}\,\text{s}\,(3\,\text{yrs )$ the BE99 method measures the true ionisation within the 5\% error margin.}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: In comparison, the hydrogen recombination time derived from Eq.\,\ref{eq:recombination_time is about $\tau_\text{rec \approx 50\,\text{yrs}$, implying $\tau_\text{BE < \tau_\text{rec << \tau_\text{eq}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: In conclusion, neither $ \tau_\text{eq}$ nor $\tau_\text{rec}$ are indicating the relevant time scale for the applicability of the BE99 method -- it is rather $\tau_\text{BE}$.
elements.sent-2411-140: We tested this hypothesis by integrating 192 models with different input parameters (Fig.\,\ref{fig:be99_time ).}
elements.sent-2411-141: All the tested models have a BE99 equilibrium time smaller than $10^{9}\,\text{s}\approx 32\,\text{yrs}$.
elements.sent-2411-142: This result supports the notion that the BE99 equilibrium is reached fast enough -- even faster that the hydrogen recombination time.

elements.section-2411-003: The BE99 method with more line ratios (BE99e)
